# How to use this template project


Write your Markdown as you would in any other Quarto project. Text in the main body looks like this paragraph. In both HTML and PDF output, it spans the standard content width, leaving space on the exterior side of the page for marginal notes .


All of Quarto's normal features for technical writing [See Quarto documentation on [supported Markdown](https://quarto.org/docs/authoring/markdown-basics.html#footnotes).]{.column-margin}, such as footnotes and indexed labelled figures, are available .

## Footnotes

To place a footnote in your text, create an identifier using squarebrackets with a caret; start a line with the same identifier and a colon to write the content of the footnote. This markup:

```
Main body content.[^fn]

[^fn]: Footnote content.
```

produces this output:

> Main body content.[^fn]

[^fn]: Footnote content.


In the HTML rendering, hovering over the footnote marker shows its contents in a floating box. In both HTML and PDF renderings, the footnote is placed at the bottom of the page.


## Labelled figures

To create a styled labelled figure, use normal Markdown for an embedded image, and add an identifier starting with `#fig-` in curly brackets. The image will span the main text width, with a styled caption placed directly beneath the image.

For example, this Markdown produces this output:

```
![Opening of letter of Ebenezer White to Nelson White.]
(https://neelsmith.quarto.pub/personal/posts/imgs/filled-w-grief.png){#fig-ebenezer}
```



![Opening of letter of Ebenezer White to Nelson White.](https://neelsmith.quarto.pub/personal/posts/imgs/filled-w-grief.png){#fig-ebenezer}

Elsewhere in your text, you can cite the image by replacing `#` in the identifier with `@` to embed a properly labelled link. In this example, embedding the citation `@fig-ebenezer` produces this labelled link: @fig-ebenezer. In the HTML version, hovering over the link displays a small image of the figure in a floating panel.




## Citing sources

Follow standard Quarto practices to cite sources [See [Quarto documentation on citations](https://quarto.org/docs/authoring/citations.html).]{.column-margin}   using reference IDs in a BibTex bibliography. For example, if the reference ID for your source is `@tufte2001`, you can include a citation like `[@tufte2001]` to produce this citation in your text: [@tufte2001]. In both HTML and PDF output, citations are linked to full bibliographic information in the [appended bibliography](./references.qmd).


The [💡 **Tip**: if you manage your bibliography with Zotero, you can use the BetterBibTex plugin to automatically update an export of your bibliography to whatever file you configure in `_quarto.yml`.]{.column-margin} bibliographic source file is configured in `_quarto.yml` (with the line `bibliography: references.bib`). 




## Adding marginal notes

This project's configuration adds a `column-margin` class that lets you add marginal notes. Place the note in square brackets followed by `{.column-margin}`. To align the note horizontally with a specific passage of the main body text, embed the note in the text at the point you want it to align with. For example, this markup:

```
A **paragraph** [The content of your **marginal note**.]{.column-margin} 
with an aligned marginal note. The paragraph text 
continues to flow normally in the main text block.
```


produces this:

> A **paragraph** [The content of your **marginal note**.]{.column-margin} with an aligned marginal note. The paragraph text continues to flow normally in the main text block.

## Version information and project metadata

The configuration in `_quarto.yml` defines variables for `version` and `date`, and uses them in a footer for HTML pages. The same variables are used in the file [`releases.qmd`](./releases.qmd) to display the current version and date at the top of a history of releases.
